channel.title: Comments for page "Land Of Honey"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Land Of Honey"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, it's ripe with a lot fo col ways you could take early 20th century anomalies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dude, thank you! Means a lot as I and Flops were given good advice from Nico to do it in this format. Nico, thus saving another draft from "meh"ishness.

item.title: Re: Land of Honey feedback
item.content:encoded:
Oh absolutely not! I'll see what I can later when I had more time to edit.

Thank you, I love comments like these. Have a good day!

item.title: Re: Land of Honey feedback
item.content:encoded:
Well justified! Perhaps some nod to the 'nine maidens' thing would be nice, though that is perhaps just my ignorance in not being overly familiar with mythology.

Again, a really nice piece I hope my criticism wasn't perceived as harsh, I was just being nit-picky :)

item.title: Re: Land of Honey feedback
item.content:encoded:
Yeah another user had suggested that too for the second listpage though me and my partner are busy and cant go back to condense the right parts. Will definitely keep this in mind, thank you for your extensive feedback.

O'Sullivan
– hide block

I remember when we were young, we would read the posters and poems and newspapers from Father's small print shop. She was always frail and soft-spoken.

Perhaps I played up his wage work more than what was necessary but I had this sense that like many immigrants of the time, they only had what they could find as laborers primarily. Jobs that they might have not specialized in back home. Like a college graduate in literature working a McDonald's job.

I do question why 8 (+ 1) women were sacrificed by fifthists however; why not 5? I may have missed a trick here.

A) the photo had 9, and I couldn't find a decent one of 5 women for whatever reason but also;
B) https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nine_maidens_(mythology)#Ireland and https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Muses <- had it in mind to loosely reference these mythologies given its almost like "magick", from all parts of the world, comes to America.

item.title: Land of Honey feedback
item.content:encoded:
Feedback
Hide Feedback

I liked the concept for the presentation a lot but sometimes looking at the yellow pages seemed to go on a while, particularly in the second set of letters. The use of media such as the newspaper article was a really nice addition and perhaps could be use to augment and break up the longer segments of dialogue?

In the O'Sullivan letters, the use of dates might help provide an idea for scale of the time frames, it would also be unusual to send letters across the sea without dating them, I feel.

Also, I feel the use of vernacular in these first letters could help build an idea for the character more. The content seemed to set O'Sullivan up as a poor, working class Dublin boy, but I can't imagine such a person casually using words like 'obfuscate' in a letter to their parents.

During the Luca and Castelli bits, initially I thought it was bordering on cliche but I changed my mind as I read on and really enjoyed it. The phrase 'dead-Mick-storage' made me chuckle! A nice section.

I enjoyed the UIU report greatly also, I found it's presentation to be a nice change of pace compared to the letters and I personally find fifthism to be a very interesting part of the SCP universe so I enjoyed this section narratively also. I do question why 8 (+ 1) women were sacrificed by fifthists however; why not 5? I may have missed a trick here.

Bonus points for mentioning Liverpool (my hometown!) however I should mention that the University of Sheffield is in Sheffield, not Liverpool!

Where I struggle is piecing together the narrative, this is probably my own ineptitude and lack of sleep so potentially disregard this last point, but I don't quite follow what exactly happened, I follow the gist but I couldn't necessarily pull together an exact chain of events with a concise, satisfying end point.

However overall really enjoyable read, +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Beautiful use of format in storytelling!

Once again, the Foundationverse is a place of wonders and horrors.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The presentation here is extremely slick and you have a page with multiple iterations of content presented clearly and unambiguously. I found this to nail the historical tone of the time period and the story paces itself well. I found the UIU format a little underwhelming but this hits every note it sets out to play. +1

item.title: Re: JS Site Crit Team
item.content:encoded:
You make some interesting points about the prose and font use. I suppose in terms of the font, it's a toss-up between authenticity versus readability, and you can't fully have both! I hope this issue doesn't deter any readers.

On another note, I'd like to point out that the article is more than just the three letters by O'Sullivan — in fact, it's another three (3) ListPages following that (some of which use other custom fonts), as linked in the page navigation at the bottom. This feedback has made me realize that the ListPages aren't too explicit; so, I've added an out-of-universe note to the top of each page in the article. There's a lot of additional context that tells a broader story than just the letters. Hope that helps!

item.title: JS Site Crit Team
item.content:encoded:
These are well-written letters and I was able to easily slip into the imagery in my head provided by them. My only confusion is maybe some of the language choices uses by the main character? They are a bit high brow for what I would expect, but, this is easily worked around mentally with the idea that everyone viewed the main character migrant scum rather than checking his actual qualifications.

Re: the font, periods are kind of tough to make out but I didn't have any problems with the context clues in finding full stops.

item.title: Special Information!
item.content:encoded:
Readers! Please note that this Tale has FOUR different chapters. Reading all four parts is necessary to understand the full context.

Please see the navigation links at the bottom of each page to view the next part of the article!

item.title: Land of Honey
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, you fall from the land of honey
Where there's land of promise
All from the land of hunts
All from the land of light
All from the land, that's us
When you see smoke, there's fire

Land of Honey
Authors: KindlyTurtleClem and Lt Flops

This article was written by FIRE TURTLE (Clem and Floppy) for the 2019 Collaboration Contest! The two primary Groups of Interest featured are Fifthism and Church of the Second Hytoth.

Kudos to Clem for being an excellent co-author and for putting so much time and effort into this.

Go here for the second entry!

▶ Special Thanks and Image Attribution

▲ Close
